Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic this semester. I'd try to set aside time on weekends to catch up on everything I missed during the week, but it rarely worked out as planned. I'm a visual learner, so I spent a lot of time creating diagrams and mind maps from the lecture materials, which took longer than just reading. I'd usually study in bursts of a few hours when I could focus, but my anxiety made it hard to stick to any consistent schedule.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and how overwhelmed I felt. I'd have weeks where I barely touched anything, then suddenly cram 19 hours into a few days before a deadline. Being 25 and having other life responsibilities meant I couldn't always study when I wanted to. Deadlines definitely drove my schedule more than any routine I tried to establish.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or realizing I'd fallen behind again that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about new materials and that would prompt me to check. Honestly, anxiety about missing something important was often the push I needed. I'd also log in when I knew discussion posts were expected, even if I dreaded participating.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. I tried to make it a habit early in the semester, but my low attendance and the anxiety I felt about being behind made logging in feel more stressful than routine. There were maybe a couple weeks where I checked in daily, but that fell apart pretty quickly. The LMS never felt comfortable enough to become automatic for me.
Q5: The video lectures were most useful for me because I could pause, rewind, and take visual notes at my own pace. I watched over four hours of video content because that format really clicked with my learning style. The discussion boards were also helpful when other students explained concepts differently than the professor. Being able to see diagrams and visual explanations made complex topics actually stick.
Q6: I often ignored the reading materials and some of the supplementary PDFs. They felt overwhelming when I was already anxious about being behind, and walls of text don't work well for how I learn. I'd skim them at best or just rely on the videos to cover the same content. Some optional practice exercises I kept putting off because they felt like extra pressure rather than helpful.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate relevance to assignments or exams, or if it helped me visualize a concept I was struggling with. I needed to feel like I was making progress, not just going through motions. If something was interactive or visual, I was much more likely to engage fully. When I could connect it to something practical, that made all the difference.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford when I was barely keeping up with required work. There was always this guilt about not doing them, but my anxiety made me prioritize what was mandatory. Required activities had clear consequences, so they got done even when I was struggling. Optional ones felt like they were for students who had their lives together, not for me.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a ton of studying that never showed up in the LMS. I'd download videos and PDFs to study offline on my tablet, especially when I wanted to avoid the anxiety of seeing everything I hadn't completed. I used external YouTube videos to understand concepts visually when the course materials weren't clicking. My handwritten notes and diagrams were probably where most of my actual learning happened.
Q10: There were many times I'd print out materials or screenshot diagrams and study from those for hours without touching the LMS. I'd work through problems in a notebook, rewatching downloaded videos multiple times. Sometimes I'd study with a friend who was in the same course, going over concepts together, but none of that collaboration showed up anywhere. My assignment scores being so high came from that invisible work.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a video or document once to download it, then spend hours with it offline. My internet is fine, but studying offline reduced my anxiety about seeing all the incomplete tasks on the dashboard. I preferred having materials on my own device where I could organize them visually the way that made sense to me. The LMS would show one brief access, but I might have spent three hours with that material.
Q12: There were definitely periods where my anxiety spiked and I just couldn't face the course at all. When I'd see how far behind I was in attendance, it felt paralyzing rather than motivating. Mid-semester was particularly rough when assignments started piling up and I felt like I was drowning. The CBT sessions I did helped me work through some of that, but the damage to my attendance was already done.
Q13: My anxiety was the biggest factor affecting my LMS use, more than workload honestly. When I felt overwhelmed, I'd avoid logging in entirely because seeing my progress felt too stressful. Fatigue from worrying about the course ironically made me less able to actually engage with it. Competing responsibilities gave me excuses to avoid the platform when my anxiety was high.
Q14: Many times I'd log in and just stare at the screen, clicking through materials without absorbing anything. My mind would be racing with anxiety about everything I needed to do instead of focusing on what was in front of me. I'd watch videos but realize I hadn't retained anything because I was too stressed. Those sessions felt productive in the moment but were basically empty engagement.
Q15: I postponed exam preparation constantly, even knowing it was crucial. The anxiety made me avoid things that felt high-stakes, which is probably why my exam score was so low despite my assignment work being strong. I'd tell myself I'd study tomorrow when I felt less anxious, but that day rarely came. It's frustrating because I knew the material when I engaged with it properly.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to submit work. I'd have intense bursts of activity right before something was due, which is probably visible in my response times. Without a deadline, I'd keep putting things off indefinitely. The pressure helped override my avoidance tendencies, but it also made the work more stressful than it needed to be.
Q17: When I got good feedback on assignments, it actually helped reduce my anxiety temporarily and motivated me to engage more. Seeing that my work was valued made the course feel less threatening. But if feedback was delayed, I'd spiral into worrying about whether I'd done something wrong. The positive feedback on my assignments was probably what kept me from giving up entirely.
Q18: The structure felt overwhelming at times because there was so much to keep track of. Having clear modules helped, but seeing everything laid out also triggered my anxiety about how much I was missing. I needed more flexibility in deadlines given my situation, but I also know I might have just procrastinated more with that freedom. It's complicated.
Q19: The video content being available on-demand made engagement much easier for me as a visual learner. What made it harder was the dashboard showing all my incomplete items every time I logged in - that was anxiety-inducing. The discussion requirements felt forced and uncomfortable for someone who prefers to learn independently. More visual summaries and fewer text-heavy readings would have helped me a lot.
Q20: I can totally relate to that pattern. Sometimes you're logging in repeatedly trying to understand something, watching the same video multiple times, participating in discussions, but still not producing finished work. Anxiety can make you engage with materials obsessively without being able to complete the final step of submitting. High activity might actually signal struggle rather than success.
Q21: That's basically my story with assignments. My LMS engagement was low, but my assignment scores were nearly perfect because I did most of my real learning offline. I'd download what I needed and work intensively outside the system. The LMS only captured a fraction of my actual study time. Someone looking at my logs would never guess I was putting in 19 hours a week.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when you're active and what you click on, but they completely miss the quality and depth of offline engagement. They can't capture handwritten notes, downloaded materials being studied, or learning that happens through conversation with peers. They also can't distinguish between anxious clicking and genuine engagement. The numbers tell a very incomplete story.
Q23: Being engaged means actually wrestling with the material until it makes sense, not just completing checkboxes. It's when I'm drawing diagrams, making connections, asking myself questions about the content. For me, engagement is mostly internal and visual - it's happening in my head and my notebooks. True engagement is when I care about understanding, not just passing.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much my anxiety affected my experience, or how the CBT sessions actually helped me cope better toward the end. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with downloaded materials, the visual notes I created, or the relief I felt when assignments went well. It definitely wouldn't show that despite failing, I actually learned a lot - I just couldn't demonstrate it under exam pressure.
